metadata.summary: Configure budget alerts at 50/75/90/100% thresholds, automate anomaly investigation, and use structured LLM prompts for cost analysis, rightsizing, commitment, and executive reporting workflows.

content.title: FinOps Budget Alerts, Anomaly Investigation, and Cost Reporting
content.summary: Budget alerts at 50/75/90/100% thresholds prevent surprise overspend by notifying teams before the billing period closes. Anomaly detection catches intra-period spikes (new deployment, configuration error, traffic event) within hours. Structured LLM prompts accelerate cost analysis, rightsizing review, commitment planning, and executive reporting — reducing FinOps analyst time per cycle from days to hours.

why.p: Cloud bills are retrospective by default — you see what you spent after the month closes. Budget alerts and anomaly detection shift this to near-real-time: teams learn about a cost spike within hours, while the causal deployment or configuration is still fresh and reversible. Without alerts, a misconfigured NAT Gateway or forgotten GPU instance runs for 30 days before anyone notices.

LLM prompts in FinOps workflows compress the analyst bottleneck: a structured cost analysis prompt that takes a billing CSV as input and returns ranked optimization opportunities in 60 seconds replaces 2 hours of pivot-table work. The value compounds across weekly reviews.

list.item: Setting up any cloud account — budget alerts at all four thresholds should be Day 1 infrastructure, not an afterthought.
Monthly billing review — use LLM prompts to generate the initial analysis, then verify the top recommendations manually.
A cost spike appears in anomaly detection — use the investigation prompt to structure the root-cause analysis.
Preparing quarterly commitment reviews (Savings Plans / RIs) — use the commitment prompt to structure the analysis.
Generating executive or team-level cost reports — prompts produce consistent format across reporting periods.
Replacing human judgment for commitment purchases — LLM prompts surface candidates and trade-offs; a human must approve multi-thousand-dollar commitments.
Auto-remediating anomalies without an approval step — auto-termination of a resource identified by anomaly detection can cause an incident if misclassified.

rule.statement: Every cloud account, subscription, or project with measurable spend MUST have budget alerts at 50%, 75%, 90%, and 100% of monthly budget. The 50% and 75% alerts notify the team lead. The 90% and 100% alerts escalate to engineering leadership. Alerts MUST be "actual spend" for 50-90% and both "actual" and "forecasted" for 100% (catch overspend before it happens).
rule.rationale: Four-threshold alerting gives increasing urgency signal: 50% is informational, 75% is attention, 90% is action required, 100% is escalation. Forecasted alerts at 100% give 5-10 days lead time to intervene before actual overspend.

example.title: AWS Budget Terraform with 4 thresholds
example.note: Tag-filtered budget per project. Use consolidated billing account-level budget as a backstop. Azure and GCP equivalents: azurerm_consumption_budget_resource_group and google_billing_budget.

example.title: Azure Budget Terraform

rule.statement: Enable native anomaly detection on all cloud accounts: AWS Cost Anomaly Detection, Azure Cost Management alerts, GCP Billing budget alerts with threshold type CURRENT_SPEND. Set anomaly alert threshold to 20% above 7-day moving average for services over $500/month. When an anomaly triggers, investigate within 4 hours using the structured investigation checklist: check CloudTrail/Activity Log for resource changes, review usage metrics for the anomalous service, identify the deployment or configuration event that caused it.
rule.rationale: 4-hour investigation window limits blast radius: a misconfigured data transfer route or forgotten GPU instance costs $50-500/hour. Acting within 4 hours caps overspend; waiting until end-of-month can mean $10k+ surprises.

example.title: Anomaly investigation template
example.code:
## Cost Anomaly Investigation
Anomaly Details: - Date detected: - Provider: - Service: - Account/Subscription: - Expected daily cost: - Actual daily cost: - Variance %:
Resource Changes (from CloudTrail/Activity Log): | Resource | Change Type | Timestamp | Changed By |
Root Cause (check one): [ ] New deployment [ ] Configuration change [ ] Traffic spike [ ] Data transfer increase [ ] Storage growth [ ] Pricing change [ ] Attack/abuse
Resolution: | Action | Owner | Status | Completion Date |
Prevention: [ ] Add monitoring/alert [ ] Update budget [ ] Implement guardrail [ ] Update runbook
example.note: Complete this template within 4 hours of anomaly alert. Store in incident postmortem system. Review prevention checklist before closing.

section.name: llm-prompts-cost-analysis
section.p: LLM prompts for FinOps work best when they receive structured data (CSV exports, usage tables, resource lists) alongside a specific output format request. The prompts below cover the most common recurring FinOps analyst tasks.

prompt.purpose: Analyze monthly cloud bill for optimization opportunities

section.prompt:
Analyze these EC2/VM instances and provide rightsizing recommendations:
Instance data: | Instance ID | Type | vCPUs | Memory | Avg CPU % | Avg Mem % | Peak CPU % | Peak Mem % | Monthly Cost | {instance_table}
Consider: - Minimum 20% headroom for peaks - Instance family alternatives (compute, memory, general) - ARM-based options where applicable
Provide: 1. Instances that should be downsized 2. Instances that should use different families 3. Estimated savings for each recommendation 4. Migration risks and considerations 5. Priority order for implementation

Analyze this usage data and recommend Savings Plans commitments:
Current state: - Monthly On-Demand compute spend: ${amount} - Existing Savings Plans: {current_commitments} - Current coverage: {percentage}%
Usage pattern (last 6 months): | Month | Total Compute | Min Daily | Max Daily | Avg Daily | {usage_table}
Recommend: 1. Optimal hourly commitment amount 2. Compute SP vs EC2 Instance SP split 3. 1-year vs 3-year term recommendation 4. Expected savings and break-even analysis 5. Ramp-up strategy (if starting fresh) 6. Risk assessment

rule.statement: Establish the following review cadence: daily anomaly alert review (5 min triage), weekly team cost review (use team cost report prompt + actual CUR/billing data), monthly FinOps team analysis (executive summary prompt), quarterly commitment optimization review (Savings Plan/RI prompt), annually strategy and tooling review. Log each review outcome and action items in a shared FinOps tracker.
rule.rationale: Regular cadence prevents FinOps from becoming a reactive firefight. Daily triage catches spikes; weekly reviews catch drift; monthly summaries maintain executive visibility; quarterly reviews optimize commitments before terms renew.

checklist.check: Enable AWS Cost Anomaly Detection for services over $500/month with 20% threshold.
Deploy budget alerts at 50%, 75%, 90%, 100% for every account/subscription/project.
Configure separate escalation path (engineering lead) for 90%+ alerts.
Enable billing exports: AWS CUR, Azure Cost Management, GCP BigQuery export.
Set up anomaly investigation runbook linked from alert notifications.
Prepare LLM prompt templates pre-filled with account/project context for reuse in weekly reviews.
Establish weekly team cost review meeting with team cost report prompt as first input.
Run monthly executive summary using the executive summary prompt on actual billing data.
Quarterly: run Savings Plan commitment prompt against last 6 months of usage data.
